Inaugural vol: 'Trinitarian Ontologies: Towards a Trinitarian Transformation of Philosophy.' Perichoresis in contemporary metaphysics: 'Triune God is the ontological ground of relationality itself — being is relational because God is relation.' 2026: Proposal 'Trilism' — new systems theory with triadic ontological structure: Spiritual World, Mediator, Material World (PhilArchive). PhilArchive paper: 'Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics' (philarchive.org/archive/MIGROA-2).

ai_systems.findings: 2026 defined by architecture over model intelligence. Multi-agent orchestration now standard for enterprise AI. Key insight: mixing models (frontier for reasoning, lightweight for volume tasks) reduces costs 40–60%. Top frameworks: LangGraph (stateful workflows), AutoGen (multi-agent conversations), CrewAI (role-based), OpenAgents (financial), MetaGPT (software development). MAS-Orchestra: function-calling RL framework for holistic multi-agent orchestration; introduces MASBENCH evaluation. NextBigFuture: '2026 is Breakthrough Year for Reliable AI World Models and Continual Learning Prototypes.' Agentic AI era: dynamic model routing is must-have; most multi-agent failures stem from weak reasoning architecture, not weak models.

uap.findings: February 2026: Trump signed executive order directing UAP records declassification — 300-day deadline. Agencies must release materials, provide redacted versions, or submit reviewable justifications for continued classification. Described as 'most significant executive action on UAP transparency in US history.' Congressional hearings: Joint subcommittee hearing 'Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena: Exposing the Truth'; 'Restoring Public Trust Through UAP Transparency and Whistleblower Protection' (examining AARO). FY2026 NDAA: Pentagon must brief lawmakers on UAP intercepts by integrated military commands since 2004. H.R.1187 UAP Transparency Act introduced in 119th Congress. FBI investigating deaths/disappearances of 10+ scientists with govt ties (some online speculation links to UAP programs); incl. retired USAF Maj. Gen. McCasland (disappeared Albuquerque, Feb 2026). IAEA chief: peace without nuclear oversight is an 'illusion.'

newyorker.title: The Anatomy of a Failure
newyorker.dek: From spray-on condoms to radioactive wrinkle cream, “Flops?!” at the Musée des Arts et Métiers, in Paris, puts terrible inventions in the spotlight.
newyorker.text:
“We thought it would be interesting to show that which we never show,” Marjolaine Schuch, who co-curated “Flops?!,” explained recently. “All the stuff that gets shoved under the carpet.” The show originated at a design museum in Saint-Étienne; Musée des Arts et Métiers has supplemented it with lemons from its permanent collection, such as the ten-hour decimal-time clock, used during the French Revolution. “This is the Pascaline,” Schuch said, pointing to what looked like a jewelry box covered in brass gears—an early, not entirely dependable, calculating machine conceived by Blaise Pascal. In mounting the show, Schuch and her team committed a gaffe of their own. “We didn’t include the dates of the inventions, and we immediately started getting all kinds of comments,” she confessed. The Pascaline’s label bore evidence of their cleanup job: a rubber-stamped addition reading “1642.”
Nearby, a screen displayed e-mails between the investors Paul Graham and Fred Wilson: “I am not sure they can take on the hotel market,” Wilson writes, deciding against investing in Airbnb. Graham published the exchange on his blog in 2011, as a testament to the fact that even successful people don’t always get it right. That such a blunder might be shrugged off and even celebrated, rather than shamefully shunted away, struck Schuch as a very American notion. (Anyone who has witnessed a French schoolchild’s horror of committing “fautes” will agree.) “In France, we never talk about these steps, this groundwork, these impasses,” Schuch said. “This was an opportunity to demystify and de-dramatize failure, this idea that making a mistake is something rather serious from which one doesn’t necessarily recover.”
Not every downfall has an upside, as early adopters of Tho-Radia, a line of thorium- and radium-based face creams, promoted by Dr. Alfred Curie—no relation to Marie—soon learned. And then there’s London’s “Walkie-Talkie” building: a skyscraper whose convex shape caught the sun like a magnifying glass, melting cars. In addition to such “dangerous flops,” the exhibit features inventions that fizzled because they were expensive and unnecessary (the Juicero juicing machine), poorly designed (the Vasa, a Swedish warship that sunk on its maiden voyage), or simply ahead of their time (La Jamais Contente, a speedy electric car that came out in 1899). “Everybody forgets that electric vehicles were invented at the same time as combustion-powered vehicles,” Schuch said. A case nearby contained an old Michelin guide, listing charging stations in every city.
Of all the categories of fuckups, perhaps the most jaw-dropping is the “faux pas,” demonstrating, according to the museum’s literature, “at best tactlessness, and, at worst, bad intentions.” A crowd gawked at such specimens as a hollow golf club that one can urinate into and the pastel-toned Bic for Her ballpoint pen, designed “to fit comfortably into a woman’s hand” (though perhaps not into her wallet, as the product cost up to seventy per cent more than non-gendered pens). In 2014, Zara released a blue-and-white-striped child’s shirt embellished with a yellow star. It was meant to evoke a sheriff’s uniform, but, for many, it called to mind concentration camps.
Schuch led the way through a room wallpapered with whimsical non-starters dreamed up by the artist Jacques Carelman: a sinuous Ping-Pong table, transparent playing cards. A section devoted to transportation offered such wonders of disappointment as a shuttle bus, proposed for the La Défense district near Paris, that progressed twice as slowly as a person on foot. The food-processing sector proved equally inadequate: Colgate lasagna, anyone? Schuch paused in front of a Tetra Pak carton of Côtes du Rhône, noting that while such packaging had gone over fine in Nordic countries, French consumers had rejected it vehemently. “Wine in a carton,” she said, shaking her head. “It’s impossible, psychologically.”
In the final gallery, a line had formed in front of [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: Caught in the Crackdown
enriched_articles.text: Over 1/3 of 300+ arrests at Trump immigration sweeps collapsed — charges dismissed or defendants acquitted (vs. 90%+ typical federal conviction rate). Key case: Marine vet Alejandro Orellana arrested for distributing water/face shields; charges dropped within weeks. Border Patrol chief Gregory Bovino instructed agents to 'arrest as many people that touch you.' Cole Sheridan (Chicago): falsely accused of assaulting Bovino; dismissed after video surfaced. Expert federal defender: 'We've never had a situation where it seems like you arrest first and then try to justify the reasons later.' Even failed prosecutions produce chilling effect — arrested individuals report lasting trauma and job loss from federal charges and DHS social media posts.

enriched_articles.title: FBI Investigating Possible Links Between Deaths and Disappearances of At Least 10 Scientists
enriched_articles.text: FBI investigating potential connections among deaths/disappearances of 10+ scientists with ties to government projects or sensitive information. Prompted by Trump and Republican lawmakers highlighting cases. FBI coordinating with Dept of Energy, Pentagon, state/local law enforcement. House Oversight Committee also investigating. Most recent case: retired USAF Maj. Gen. William Neil McCasland disappeared from Albuquerque home, February 2026. McCasland's wife: 'quite unlikely that he was taken to extract very dated secrets' (retired 13 years ago). No confirmed connections beyond government affiliations. No cases before 2022. NASA: 'nothing related to NASA indicates a national security threat.' Dept of Energy examining nuclear scientist cases. Some online speculation links McCasland to 'To the Stars' UAP organization — wife dismissed link.

enriched_articles.title: 2026 Ballot Measures: 4 Reforms Targeting Campaign Finance and Dark Money
enriched_articles.text: Fetch failed (403). Summary from search: 4 ballot measures in Alaska, California, and Missouri targeting campaign finance reform and dark money in November 2026 elections. California legislature referred statute to repeal 1988 ban on public campaign financing. OpenSecrets launched 'State of Disclosure' series examining enforcement across all 50 states.

enriched_articles.title: Everyone Is Blaming AI for the Water Crisis. We're Looking at the Wrong Culprit
enriched_articles.text: Environmental policy expert challenges AI water-blame narrative with empirical field data. Finding: researcher's one drive to Colorado River field work used more than 20x the water of all AI use over 11 weeks. Conclusion: personal activities and travel dwarf AI's water footprint. AI is a convenient scapegoat for systemic water issues — conventional human activities represent the more significant consumption concern. Source: Outside Online article featuring Colorado River environmental specialist's field measurements.

enriched_articles.title: The UAP Disclosure Act Timeline — 2023 to 2026
enriched_articles.text: Timeline from David Grusch July 2023 Congressional testimony (alleged US govt 'multi-decade UAP crash retrieval/reverse-engineering program') through 2026. Dec 2023: UAP Disclosure Act proposed as NDAA amendment (Presidential Review Board modeled on JFK Records Act). June 2024: AARO historical findings contradicted Grusch claims. February 2026: Trump EO directing UAP records declassification — 300-day deadline; agencies release, redact, or justify. Described as 'most significant executive action on UAP transparency in US history.' Outcome contingent on review board independence — risk of functioning as delay tactic vs. genuine disclosure.
